summary: During World War II, the whole country was patriotic and eager to serve. I joined the Marines in early 1944, right in the middle of the war. The war shaped everything about my training and deployment, especially in the Pacific theater. It was a time when young men like me felt a strong duty to participate and contribute.

I joined the United States Marine Corps when I was seventeen because I was drawn to the Marines' role in the Pacific War. Boot camp was tough but transformative, designed to strip away civilian habits and build us into Marines. I trained at Camp Pendleton and later fought with the 4th Marine Division. The Marines taught me discipline, camaraderie, and how to survive in battle.

The Pacific War was the main reason I wanted to join the Marines. We were constantly aware of the battles happening on islands like Tarawa, Saipan, and Iwo Jima. The training and preparation we received were all geared toward fighting in the Pacific. It was a brutal campaign, and many of my fellow Marines, like Larry Bonestrasser, lost their lives in those battles.

Larry Bonestrasser was one of the friends I joined the Marines with. Unfortunately, he was killed in Okinawa, which was a harsh reminder of the dangers we faced. We started this journey together, but he didn’t make it back. His loss stayed with me throughout the war.

I was part of the Battle of Iwo Jima, which was one of the fiercest battles in the Pacific War. It happened after my training and deployment in 1944. The battle tested everything we learned in boot camp and on the range. It was a defining moment for me and many Marines in the 4th Marine Division.

I remember the Attack on Pearl Harbor clearly, even though I was young at the time. It was a Sunday afternoon in 1941, and my family was listening to a football game when the news broke. That attack was the catalyst for the U.S. entering the war, and it motivated many of us to enlist, including myself.

John O'Connor was a Marine I met on the train to San Diego. He was remarkable because he joined the Marines at just fifteen years old, which was unusual. We kept in touch over the years, and he even sent me a copy of his service records. His story showed me how young some of us were when we went off to war.
cues: met on train to San Diego

summary: Milwaukee was where I went for my medical examination before boot camp. They had set up a factory building as a medical center, and it was there that volunteers and draftees were sorted out. It was a significant step in the process of becoming a Marine. I remember the sergeant calling out who was assigned to the Marines and who to the Navy.

During our train rides to boot camp, the Red Cross ladies greeted us at stops with doughnuts and coffee. It was a small kindness that meant a lot to us young Marines who were seeing new places for the first time. Their support helped boost morale as we traveled to California for training.

Tarawa was a brutal battle that our drill instructor had fought in and was wounded during. He showed us his wounds and shared stories to prepare us mentally for what was ahead. The battle was a harsh lesson in the realities of war, and it influenced how tough our training was. It was clear they wanted us ready for the worst.

Bill Compton was a fellow Marine and a full-blooded Indian from the Dakotas. He was an outstanding warrior who loved the battle, always among the first to charge. Sadly, he was one of the first to be killed. His courage and spirit left a strong impression on me.

The Dakotas were home to some of the Marines I served with, like Bill Compton. It was interesting to meet men from such different parts of the country, including the Dakotas. Their backgrounds added to the diverse makeup of our unit and the shared experience of the war.

Ray Smith was the sergeant of my unit in the 4th Marine Division. He was a true southern gentleman from Florida and a dedicated leader. During battle, seeing his helmet above the foxhole meant I was in good hands. He always kept his head up and looked out for his men, which gave us confidence.

The Rape of Nanking was mentioned during our training to illustrate the brutality of the Japanese soldiers. We learned that they practiced bayonet drills on live prisoners, which was horrifying. This knowledge made our training with bayonets and weapons feel even more serious and necessary. It was a stark reminder of the enemy we faced.

Manila was another place where the Japanese committed atrocities during the war, similar to what happened in Nanking. We heard about these events during training, which underscored the harshness of the enemy. It helped prepare us mentally for the kind of fight we were in. The stories of Manila were part of the grim reality we faced.

China was referenced in our training as a place where the Japanese fought fiercely and committed terrible acts against prisoners. The stories of the Chinese resistance and Japanese cruelty were part of the background we learned about. It gave us a broader understanding of the war in the Pacific and the stakes involved.

During boot camp, we learned to shoot with the M1 Garand rifle, which was new to me. We started with .22 rifles and worked our way up to the M1s. It was a big step from never having held a rifle before to becoming proficient with such a powerful weapon. The rifle range week was intense but essential for combat readiness.

The USS Ranger (CV-4) wasn't directly mentioned in my story, but it was part of the broader naval operations during the war. Our transport and support often involved Navy carriers like the Ranger. The Navy and Marines worked closely together in the Pacific campaigns.

The North Sea didn't come up in my experiences, as my service was focused on the Pacific theater. I was more involved in island battles than in European or Atlantic operations. So, the North Sea was outside my area of service during the war.

San Diego was where I took the train to boot camp and met other Marines like John O'Connor. It was the gateway to our Marine training in California. The journey there was filled with anticipation and the start of our transformation into Marines.

After boot camp, we were sent to Pearl Harbor as part of the 68th Replacement Draft. Pearl Harbor was a major staging area for Marines heading into the Pacific battles. Being there made the war feel very real and imminent. It was a key point before heading into combat zones like Iwo Jima.

The Marianas Islands were part of the campaign we were involved in after training. Battles like Saipan and Tinian in the Marianas were crucial in the Pacific War. We trained hard to prepare for these island assaults, which were tough and costly. The Marianas were stepping stones toward Japan.

The Battle of Saipan was one of the major battles in the Marianas that we prepared for and participated in. It was a fierce fight that tested our training and resolve. Saipan was critical in gaining a foothold closer to Japan. The experience shaped much of my combat understanding.

Tinian was another island in the Marianas where we fought after Saipan. It was part of the strategic push toward Japan. The battle there was tough, and our training at Camp Pendleton helped us get through it. Tinian was important for airfields used later in the war.

Maui was mentioned as part of the places we saw or passed through during training or leave. It was one of the Hawaiian Islands near Pearl Harbor. Seeing places like Maui was part of the new experiences we had as Marines traveling across the country and Pacific.

The Marshall Islands were part of the Pacific campaign context in which we operated. Though I didn’t mention fighting there directly, they were part of the island-hopping strategy. The training and preparation we had were meant to prepare us for battles in places like the Marshalls.

Camp Pendleton was where we set up Tent Camp One after arriving in California. It was a rough start with no showers and bathing in a stream, but it was part of our toughening process. We learned to use various weapons and conducted night patrols in the nearby mountains. Pendleton was where we became real Marines ready for combat.

I had a few weekends of leave in Los Angeles during training, around May. We visited places like the Rose Bowl and went bowling. It was a nice break from the rigors of training and a chance to see a big city. Those weekends gave me a glimpse of civilian life before heading back to the Marines.

During my leave in Los Angeles, I visited the Rose Bowl. It was one of the sights we saw on our weekends off from training. The Rose Bowl was a symbol of home and normal life, contrasting with the military world I was in. It was a memorable part of my time before deployment.
